content.subContentText: I had planned a lot of tasks to complete going into spring break because I had been ignoring a lot of errands and projects since I started Capstone this semester. While I wasn't feeling well, I wasn't willing to throw the day away either. I picked the easiest task out of my list which was to setup a domain name I bought a month prior but had been too busy to setup. I run a lot of infrastructure for my friend group but had been serving all of that data by handing out IP addresses to people, which is clumsy and can't be secured using SSL. I got the domain running, pointed it to a few of my VPS servers using subdomains, and setup Nginx as a reverse proxy. Finally, I used LetsEncrypt to get HTTPS working, which was long overdue. I also got WireGuard running on the VPS and my local server, which I'm using to punch through my ISP's CG-NAT. It's a pretty simple setup, I just have UFW forward packets to the WireGuard interface on the VPS. Suprisingly, it's performant enough to handle game servers!

content.subContentHeading: Sunday the 29th: Get Back To Work!
content.subContentText: I wanted to get one task done per day of break at least. My car was a couple hundred miles overdue for an oil change, and considering how hard I abuse it I really needed to get that done. Plus, the oil arrived in the mail so it was a great day to do it. Simple job, jack up the car, drain the oil, replace the filter, refill, and rotate the tires while it's up. Thing is, there were also some suspension noises I had been neglecting for a few thousand miles. It seemed like a good idea to give the suspension a once over out of self preservation, so I checked out all the suspension and steering joints. Everything in the front was worn out, even the ball joints. That was pretty scary even if they weren't THAT bad yet. Well, what a wonderful excuse to upgrade the entire front end of the car! I had been itching to retrofit rack and pinion steering onto the car to significantly improve the steering feel, and that would be a lot easier to do if I replaced the front cradle with an aftermarket tubular one. I did a lot of shopping and ordered tubular control arms, stiffer springs, weight jacks, and a tubular cradle for the car. Not a cheap day, but pretty exciting!

Last semester I began work designing new suspension uprights for the car using C7 Corvette wheel hubs but never had the time to finish them. I wanted to do that so that I could shorten the steering arms to facilitate switching to a steering rack without increasing the car's turning radius. It also would have the side benefit of raising the wheel rotation axis by about an inch so I could improve the angle of the lower control arms. Well, now was the time to finish them. After I got home from work, I spent rest of the day working on the design until it got to the point where I could 3D print mock up parts to make sure everything would fit right. I got half the parts started on my printer just before going to bed after seeing that the print would take about 9 hours to complete. I have a lot of trust in my printer (I've done 30+ hour prints on it before) so this didn't concern me at all, which turned out to be a mistake.

content.subContentHeading: Tuesday the 31st: Warped Prints and 3D Scanning
content.subContentText: Well I woke up and the print had failed. Not fun and a waste of plastic, but nothing I had never dealt with before. I cleared the printer and reset the print, then pulled my car in again to get some 3D scanning done. I already had point cloud data for the front right suspension upright from when I upgraded the brakes to 6 piston Brembos a couple years ago, but I was going to need data for the entire front suspension and frame geometry to properly design the rack conversion parts. I jacked up the front end of the car again, pulled off the front wheels, popped the hood, and got to work! I don't have one of those fancy structured light scanners because they cost way too much money, so I use photogrammetry instead. Basically, you take a ton of pictures of the subject from all possible angles and throw them into an algorithm which back-calculates where the camera must have been for each shot, then triangulates points to generate a point cloud. Very cool, and very compute intensive. I ended up taking about 1,700 images and threw them into RealityScan, which struggled to handle that many inputs on my machine. It failed to align all the images, creating clusters of point clouds which I needed to painstakingly align by manually annotating as many images as possible. I had to do that probably five times, which was especially painful because even though I built my machine with RealityScan in mind each iteration still took about an hour of processing time on top of hours of work spent on annotation. While waiting for the scans to process, I would check up on the printer. More warping. Jam. Warping again. I never have problems like this, but it had been a long time since I attempted a print this big and my machine is getting pretty worn out. I slowed down my print settings and set the printer to run overnight as it was getting very late and I needed to wake up in the morning to go to work, even though I knew it was risky.

I woke up that day to find more printer spaghetti. I was annoyed, but I figured whatever I'll just clear it and try again before I have to go to work. While rushing to clear the plastic spaghetti, I didn't notice that the printer had also jammed. I didn't have time to sit around waiting for the bed to heat up to see how the first layer went so there was no way for me to discover the issue after missing it the first time. I just went to work and hoped it would be fine, but when I got home there was nothing printed at all because of the jam. Frustrating, but thats what I get for rushing. I cleared the jam and tried again. Three hours in, parts were warping off the bed AGAIN. I adjusted the print settings again and started another print to run overnight. Not a very productive day, but I guess I made money at work at least.

content.subContentHeading: Thursday the 2nd: An Entire Day of 3D Scanning
content.subContentText: Again I woke up to another printer jam. This jam ended up being really bad. The filament had melted within the heat brake, making it extremely difficult to clear. I ended up having to dismantle the hot end and use my smallest nozzle on my hot air station on full blast to force the plastic out. At this point I was feeling pretty over the printer, but I'm not one to give up that easily. I reset the print and decided to get to work importing the point cloud from Tuesday into CAD. I simplified the point cloud using MeshLab and imported it into SolidWorks, which went well. I defined reference planes and started placing points on critical locations like mounting bolt positions for the cradle. Something wasn't right. The dimensions didn't seem plausible. I opened RealityScan and looked at my scaling constraints, only to find that they had failed to apply. I've ran into this issue before, it happens when RealityScan attempts to use EXIF geotagging data to determine scaling. For some reason that takes precedence over your manually measured constraints, which is probably great if you're scanning entire buildings but not at this scale. Annoyingly, I disabled this the last time this happened and I always check to make sure the setting is still off while starting a new project, but I updated RealityScan halfway through the scanning process hoping that it would align the images better on Tuesday. When I did that it reset that setting and I didn't notice. Another hour of processing and another hour of pruning points and simplification ensued. Perfect time to check up on the printer again! More warping. I decided to cut my losses with the printer as it was just wasting too much time and the uprights were not critical to the project. I figured I would just have to make due with suboptimal turning radius for a little bit. Break was more than halfway over and this damn car was way over it's time budget. I still had homework to do, Capstone tasks to work on, Web Programming work, Mobile Programming wasn't even started, an embedded systems project well behind schedule, and lots of other things that needed to get done. I'm was freaking out and more stressed than I had been during most of the semester at a time when I'm supposed to be relaxing. I decided Capstone was most important, I'll work on that while the point cloud regenerates - oh it's done nevermind back to CAD! If any of my Capstone teammates are reading this I'm very sorry, I really should have cut my losses sooner. I got it in SolidWorks, and it looked good this time! Oh, and the cradle and control arms arrived in the mail! I decided I'd better scan those while the sun is still up. More hours of scanning and point cloud generation followed as I continued to neglect my more important tasks. I get the cradle into SolidWorks and it looks great! I stay up late getting an assembly together with the two point clouds and line up a model for the engine in just the right spot just before bed.

content.subContentHeading: Friday the 3rd: Gemma4 and a BMW Rack
content.subContentText: Last summer I wrote a program during spare time at work which automates invoice annotation by cutting up raw document scans and feeding them into Gemini. My coworkers were very happy that they wouldn't have to do that tedious work anymore and it was a fun little project for me. I've been maintaining it in the months since, during which I had to switch it to use Gemma3 because Google greatly tightened their rate limits on Gemini. The performance was quite a bit worse, but servicable. Gemma4 released and I was excited to hook it into my program. The results were really good especially for a 31b model. It still makes some mistakes consistently (with some funny details in the reasoning logs, it seemed to believe that the current year is 2025 and that my invoices must somehow be from the future. I started injecting the current date into my prompt and that sorted that), but overall a great improvement. While I was at work I ordered the cheapest steering rack I could find from a 2005 BMW 330ci since it seemed like it might work and took it home. It was too dark outside to scan the rack so I decided to work on designing motor mounts for the new cradle. I could have bought mounts with the cradle, but I didn't think they would fit because I mounted the engine further back than most swap kits do. After some SolidWorks wrangling the mounts look great and it was time for bed.

content.subContentHeading: Saturday the 4th: Rack Trouble, a (somewhat) SUCCESSFUL 3D PRINT, and Wait That Assignment is Due WHEN?!
content.subContentText: I very carefully unpackaged the steering rack so I could return it at work on Monday if it didn't work out and took some measurements. The measurements look promising, but I was hoping the rack would be faster than it actually was. I think they sold me a rack for a base E46 instead of one for a 330ci, but the mounting dimensions should be the same anyway so I could still scan it. I got it up on a stand and took about 600 pictures of it for RealityScan. The point cloud generation went very smoothly and I had it loaded into SolidWorks in about an hour. Another hour of CAD work and I had it lined up in just the right spot in the assembly with the other point clouds. Unfortunately, it then became clear that the turret for the steering shaft was longer than ideal and the flanges for the mounting bolts were clocked in just the wrong spot where the brackets would interfere with the engine oil pan. Oh well, time to do something else. I really needed to get prototypes printed for my motor mount design so I could mock up the cradle on an LS3 block I have waiting to be built, that way I know they will work before spending money having the designs laser cut and bent. I decided that I was gonna figure out this damn printer once and for all. I tightened up every eccentric in the X and Y axis to eliminate slop and decided to remove my FR-4 fiberglass bed and try the factory glass bed. Normally everything sticks to FR-4 like crazy, so strongly in fact that the top layer of the fiberglass is sacrificed during part removal, so I didn't think switching back to glass would help but I was desperate. I cleaned and leveled the bed and attempted to print the mounts. They still warped, but the parts were at least usable for mockup. I glued the parts together and was able to mount the block to the cradle. In fact, everything lined up perfectly, I was thrilled with the result. I deserved some ice cream after that one! I check Discord and notice that one of my teammembers for Mobile Programming said the sprint checkpoint is due on Sunday. I don't know why I thought it was due Monday, but we had not started ANYTHING. I get to work writing most of the sprint review, making a lot of assumptions for what we're going to get done the next day, and get to bed quite late.

content.subContentHeading: Sunday the 5th: An ACTUALLY Successful Print and Writing Code for Android
content.subContentText: I was determined to get the printer working properly. There was no reason for it to be struggling this hard, I knew I was missing something. I cleaned it again, and releveled the bed using feeler gauges. Still got warped parts. I releveled the bed so that the nozzle was just barely scraping it. Somehow, STILL GOT WARPING. I decided to recheck my slicer settings. Wait, why is my bed temperature so low? Was that the issue the whole time? I increase it back to 75 degrees C. No more warping. I was dumbfounded. I don't know when I changed that, but past me sure did cause me a lot of trouble. I designed some gussets to reinforce the new cradle and printed them out, which went smoothly. Anyway, I probably shouldn't have spent time doing that when the rest of the sprint review needed to get done and we had zero code written. I get the project started and get enough functionality to satisfy the requirements for the report, and our team gets it turned in with about an hour to spare. Not how I like to roll, but we got it done.

Well, that was a stressful break. I somehow got a ton of work done but barely managed complete any of my goals. I guess I was too optimistic with how quickly I thought I could get all that design work done, although all of the issues with RealityScan and the printer didn't help at all. Maybe the worst part was that I didn't even get to making any of the parts I designed in steel, so all of that rush was for nothing. I should have waited for summer and did all of the other things on my list instead (especially Capstone) but I was so tunnel visioned on getting that done that I couldn't see that I should have cut my losses and worked on something else by Thursday. Oh well, live and learn I guess.
